title: Rule-Based Systems vs Machine Learning — Spam Detection Example
overview: Compare traditional rule-based software with a machine learning approach using a spam-detection example. The notes cover problem framing, rule-based implementation and its maintenance costs, how to collect labeled data, feature extraction (binary features), training a model, producing probabilistic outputs and thresholding, and the conceptual differences between embedding logic in code versus learning logic from data.

key_concepts.title: Rule-based systems
key_concepts.description: Explicit rules are encoded in software: conditions tested on inputs produce deterministic outputs. Easy to start but brittle and expensive to maintain as patterns change.
bullet_points: Rules are written by humans after analyzing examples.
Fast to prototype but often proliferate into large, fragile codebases.
Common failure mode: overfitting rules to past patterns; frequent updates required.

key_concepts.title: Supervised machine learning
key_concepts.description: Learn a mapping from inputs (features) to outputs (labels) using training data. The learned model generalizes to new examples and outputs scores (probabilities) rather than fixed rules.
bullet_points: Requires labeled examples (e.g., spam/not-spam obtained from a 'mark as spam' button).
Features encode characteristics of inputs (can be derived from earlier rules).
Model fitting produces a reusable artifact that scores unseen data.
Produces probabilistic outputs that can be converted to actions using thresholds.

key_concepts.title: Feature engineering (binary features)
key_concepts.description: Translate email characteristics into numeric inputs for models; binary features (0/1) are common and interpretable.
bullet_points: Examples: title_length>10, body_length>200, sender_is_promotions, domain_is_online_com, body_contains_deposit.
Rules that were used in the rule-based system often suggest useful features.
Binary features simplify modeling and debugging.

key_concepts.title: Model outputs and decision thresholds
key_concepts.description: Models typically produce scores or probabilities. A decision policy maps those scores to actions (e.g., spam folder) using a threshold.
bullet_points: Threshold (e.g., 0.5) determines tradeoff between false positives and false negatives.
Adjust threshold based on business requirements (user annoyance vs missed spam).
Probabilities allow calibrated risk decisions and downstream uses (e.g., triage).

blocks.content: Goal: classify incoming emails as 'spam' or 'not spam' and route spam into a separate folder. Users may mark messages as spam, producing labels for supervised approaches.

items: Inputs: raw emails (sender, sender domain, subject, body, metadata).
Desired output: binary classification (spam/not-spam) or probability that an email is spam.
Constraints: low false-positive rate for legitimate email; adaptability as spam tactics evolve.

blocks.content: Start by analyzing spam examples and writing conditional code that checks message attributes. Example rules arise directly from observed patterns (sender domain, keywords in subject/body).

Tradeoffs:
- Pros: simple, transparent, fast to implement for obvious cases.
- Cons: rules multiply over time, interact in unexpected ways, and break as adversaries change tactics. Maintenance becomes a major cost; handling legitimate uses of flagged terms (e.g., legitimate deposit notices) requires many special-case rules.

Supervised learning requires labeled examples. For email providers, user actions such as 'mark as spam' generate labels. Use both positive (spam) and negative (not spam) examples from real traffic to train models.

items: Collect a representative dataset: millions of emails improves robustness, but a smaller seed set can start the system.
Preserve the raw input fields and the user-provided label.
Watch for label noise: users sometimes mislabel; consider cleaning or using probabilistic approaches.

blocks.content: Convert each email into a fixed-length feature vector. Start with interpretable binary features inspired by rules. Binary features: True/False encoded as 1/0.

blocks.code:
def extract_features(email):
    subject = email.get('subject','')
    body = email.get('body','')
    sender = email.get('sender','')

    features = {
        'subject_long': 1 if len(subject) > 10 else 0,
        'body_long': 1 if len(body) > 200 else 0,
        'sender_promotions_online': 1 if sender == 'promotions@online.com' else 0,
        'sender_weird': 1 if 'noreply' in sender or 'no-reply' in sender else 0,
        'domain_test_com': 1 if sender.endswith('@test.com') else 0,
        'body_contains_deposit': 1 if 'deposit' in body.lower() else 0
    }
    # Return features as ordered list matching model training
    return [features['subject_long'], features['body_long'],
            features['sender_promotions_online'], features['sender_weird'],
            features['domain_test_com'], features['body_contains_deposit']]

blocks.content: Assemble a dataset: for each labeled email, extract features and record the label (spam=1, not-spam=0). This produces X (feature matrix) and y (label vector) used for training.

items: Binary features are simple to compute and interpret, and they can be extended over time.
Many features come directly from rules you already considered — rules are a good source of features.
Complement interpretable binary features with other signal types (frequency counts, n-grams, metadata) as needed.

detailed_explanations.title: Training a model and producing probabilities

blocks.content: Train a learning algorithm on X, y to produce a model object. The model can output probabilities (confidence scores) for the 'spam' class; these probabilities are continuous and can be thresholded for decisions.

blocks.code:
# Pseudocode: generic model training and prediction
# X: list of feature vectors, y: list of labels (0/1)
model = Model()          # a placeholder for a trained model
model.fit(X, y)          # train/fit the model

# For a new email:
features = extract_features(new_email)
prob_spam = model.predict_proba([features])[0]  # probability that the email is spam

# Decision using threshold
threshold = 0.5
if prob_spam >= threshold:
    action = 'move to spam folder'
else:
    action = 'deliver to inbox'

# Note: "Model" and method names are generic placeholders and map to your chosen ML library API.

blocks.content:
Why probabilities matter:
- They capture model uncertainty and enable selective actions (e.g., quarantine only when probability is high).
- They let you tune the threshold to balance false positives and false negatives.

blocks.diagram:
flowchart TB
    subgraph RULE
      R1["Human: derive rules from examples"] --> R2["Write code encoding rules"]
      R2 --> R3["Predictions: spam or not spam (deterministic)"]
    end
    subgraph ML
      D["Labeled emails (spam/not-spam)"] --> F["Feature extraction (binary features)"]
      F --> M["Train model -> Model artifact"]
      M --> P["Model: predict probabilities for new emails"]
      P --> A["Apply threshold -> Action (spam/inbox)"]
    end

blocks.content:
Key distinctions:
- Rule-based: logic lives in code; human-maintained; brittle as patterns evolve.
- ML-based: logic is learned from data; system generalizes; requires labeled data and periodic retraining as distributions shift.

Selecting a threshold is an operational decision: a lower threshold catches more spam but risks more false positives; a higher threshold reduces false positives but may let spam through. Use validation data to choose thresholds and consider business costs for each error type.

items: Calibrate model probabilities to ensure they reflect true likelihoods (if needed).
Monitor model performance over time; retrain when performance degrades due to concept drift.
Combine ML with lightweight rules for safety nets (e.g., never delete emails from administrator addresses).

key_takeaway: Rule-based systems are fast to prototype but expensive to maintain; they encode decisions directly in code.
Supervised machine learning uses labeled data to learn a model that generalizes; features often start as the rules you originally wrote.
Binary features derived from rules provide an interpretable, effective starting point for models.
Models produce probabilities that you map to actions using thresholds — this enables flexible tradeoffs between false positives and false negatives.
project_ideas: Implement a simple rule-based spam filter in Python, then convert the same rules into binary features and train a basic classifier. Compare false positives/negatives before and after.

Build a small dataset by collecting example emails (synthetic or anonymized), label them, and experiment with different feature sets (rule-derived binaries, keyword counts, subject/body length). Investigate threshold tuning.
Create a dashboard that tracks model performance (precision, recall) over time and flags when retraining should be considered.
